_about: Measured results from the LLM prefill serving benchmark on a real TPU, run as a regime study across three real prompt-length distributions. Reproduce with notebooks/llm_serving_tpu.ipynb. cold_s = end-to-end wall-time incl. compilation (the headline cold/diverse-serving cost); warm_s +/- warm_std = steady-state over 3 hot-cache passes; speedup is cold vs the exact (per-length) baseline. Honest finding: bucketing ANY sensible way kills the recompile storm (~5-10x); the data-derived advisor MATCHES the power-of-two heuristic within ~2% on smooth real distributions rather than beating it. See docs/REPORT.md.

environment.length_source: real (tokenized from each dataset; only the length distribution is used)

headline.claim: Collapsing the recompile storm via bucketing is the win: up to 156 compiles -> 7 and ~6.7x faster on a real TPU. The advisor auto-derives a bucket set that matches the power-of-two expert heuristic within ~2% on smooth real distributions.

alpaca.label: Alpaca (short prompts)
alpaca.regime: short: mass below ~100 tokens; pow2 grid already tight

dolly.label: Dolly (spread + tail)
dolly.regime: spread: real mass across [0, max] with a tail -- the advisor's intended regime

cnn.label: CNN/DailyMail (saturated at cap)
cnn.regime: saturated: articles run to thousands of tokens, so nearly all clip to max_len -> a spike; nothing to bucket, advisor correctly picks K=1

cost_model_postmortem: The fitted compute cost is linear every run (b ~ 0 at these lengths), so the DP under-penalizes padding and over-trades it for fewer compiles. derived therefore takes fewer compiles but more padding than pow2 (e.g. dolly: 7 vs 9 compiles, 1.30x vs 1.21x pad), and the extra padding compute slightly outweighs the saved compiles -> pow2 wins by ~1-2%. A padded-token-weighted (quadratic) cost would close this gap; even then the advisor matches rather than beats pow2 on smooth distributions.
